walkthrough.title: Sort the Dice
walkthrough.text: Let's get Roll for the Galaxy set up! First, sort all the dice by color. You will see white dice, which are the starting Home dice that every player begins with. Then there are colored dice: red for military, blue for novelty goods, brown for rare elements, green for genes, yellow for alien technology, and purple for developments. Put all the colored dice in a central supply where everyone can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the VP Chip Pool
walkthrough.text: Count out victory point chips based on your player count. Use twelve VP chips per player. With two players, that is twenty-four chips. With three players, thirty-six. With four players, forty-eight. And with five players, sixty chips. Mix the one-point and five-point chips together and place them in a central pool. When this pool runs out, it triggers the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Tile Bag
walkthrough.text: Find all fifty-five game tiles and put them in the cloth draw bag. These tiles come in two types: developments, marked with a diamond icon, and worlds, marked with a circle icon. Each tile has a VP value and special abilities. Give the bag a good shake to randomize everything.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Central Area
walkthrough.text: Place the credit tokens in a central supply alongside the VP chips and dice. Put the round summary tile where everyone can see it. This tile shows the five phases in order: Explore, Develop, Settle, Produce, and Ship. It is a handy reference for what each phase does.

walkthrough.title: Deal Faction Tiles
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the nine double-sided faction tiles and deal one to each player. Each faction tile is your starting tableau. It shows your starting setup, including which dice you begin with and any initial tiles. Place your faction tile face up in front of you. Each faction has a unique starting advantage that will shape your early strategy.

walkthrough.title: Collect Starting Dice
walkthrough.text: Look at your faction tile. It tells you which dice to take as your starting dice. Most factions start with three white dice plus one or two colored dice matching their faction's specialty. Take those dice and place them in your cup. Your cup is where dice go between rounds, ready to be rolled.

walkthrough.title: Draw Starting Tiles
walkthrough.text: Your faction tile also tells you how many tiles to draw from the bag. Draw that many tiles and look at them. These are your starting options for development and settlement. You will place some under construction and return the rest to the bag, following your faction's specific instructions.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Citizenry
walkthrough.text: Any dice listed on your faction that are not in your cup start in your Citizenry. The Citizenry is the area next to your play space where unused dice wait. Dice in your Citizenry do nothing until you recruit them into your cup by paying credits. Think of them as workers waiting to be hired.

walkthrough.title: Take Your Player Screen and Phase Strip
walkthrough.text: Each player gets a player screen to hide their dice assignments and a phase strip that shows all five phases. Place the phase strip behind your screen. You will use it each round to secretly assign your dice to phases. The screen keeps your choices hidden until everyone reveals simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Collect Starting Credits
walkthrough.text: Unless your faction tile says otherwise, each player starts with one credit from the central supply. Credits are used to recruit dice from your Citizenry and are also earned through trading goods. Place your credit next to your play area where you can see it.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Roll
walkthrough.text: Everyone is set up. Roll for the Galaxy is played simultaneously, so there is no first player. Everyone rolls, assigns, and reveals at the same time. Grab your cup, give your dice a shake, and get ready for the first round!

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how Roll for the Galaxy works. This is a simultaneous engine-building game where everyone plays at the same time. Each round, you roll dice, secretly assign them to phases, choose which phase you want to activate, and then everyone reveals. Only phases that someone chose actually happen. The goal is to build the best tableau of developments and worlds while accumulating victory points.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Every round follows the same seven steps. Roll your dice behind your screen. Assign dice to phases on your phase strip. Select one die to place on your chosen phase. Everyone lifts screens simultaneously. Resolve active phases in order. Return used dice. Prepare for the next round. Let's walk through each step in detail.

walkthrough.title: Step One: Roll Your Dice
walkthrough.text: Pick up all the dice in your cup and roll them behind your screen where nobody can see the results. Each die face shows one of six symbols: Explore, Develop, Settle, Produce, Ship, or the Wild symbol, which looks like a dollar sign. The Wild face can be assigned to any phase. Different colored dice have different distributions of these symbols.

walkthrough.title: Step Two: Assign Dice to Phases
walkthrough.text: After rolling, assign each die to a phase on your phase strip. Dice must be assigned to the phase matching the symbol they rolled, unless they rolled Wild, in which case they can go to any phase. Your phase strip has five columns, one for each phase. Place each die in its matching column.

walkthrough.title: Step Three: Select Your Phase
walkthrough.text: Now pick one of your assigned dice and place it on the phase you want to activate. This is your most important decision each round. The phase you select is guaranteed to happen, regardless of what other players choose. Every other die stays where you assigned it. If the phase it was assigned to gets chosen by someone, great. If not, those dice return to your cup unused but not lost.

walkthrough.title: Step Four: Reveal
walkthrough.text: Everyone lifts their screens at the same time. Look around the table and check which phases were selected. A phase only happens if at least one player chose it. If nobody selected Produce, for example, the Produce phase is skipped entirely this round. But every player with dice assigned to an active phase gets to use those dice.

walkthrough.title: Step Five: Resolve Phases in Order
walkthrough.text: Resolve the active phases in a fixed order: Explore first, then Develop, then Settle, then Produce, then Ship. Even though play is simultaneous, resolving in order matters because tiles completed in the Develop phase are available for Settle powers and so on. Let's look at each phase.

walkthrough.title: Explore Phase
walkthrough.text: Each die you assigned to Explore lets you draw one tile from the bag and gain one credit. After drawing, you keep tiles up to the number of Explore dice you used and return the rest to the bag. Kept tiles go under construction in your play area. They are not in your tableau yet. Think of Explore as shopping for future projects and earning some spending money.

walkthrough.title: Develop Phase
walkthrough.text: Developments are tiles with a diamond icon. To build one, assign Develop dice to a development that is under construction. Each development has a die cost, which is the number of dice you need to place on it to complete it. You can build incrementally across multiple rounds. When a development has enough dice, it enters your tableau and its powers activate. The dice used are returned to your Citizenry.

walkthrough.title: Settle Phase
walkthrough.text: Worlds have a circle icon and come in two types. Non-military worlds work like developments: assign Settle dice to pay their die cost. Military worlds require red military dice equal to their defense value. Place the required dice on the world to settle it. Completed worlds enter your tableau. Some worlds are production worlds that can produce goods in the Produce phase.

walkthrough.title: Produce Phase
walkthrough.text: If you have production worlds in your tableau, you can place Produce dice on them to create goods. Each Produce die placed on an empty production world becomes a good. That die sits on the world as the good token. Each production world can hold only one good at a time. Goods are worthless until shipped.

walkthrough.title: Ship Phase
walkthrough.text: The Ship phase is where you convert goods into victory points and credits. You can trade one good for credits and VP based on the good's type. Or you can use consume powers on your tableau tiles to convert goods into VP chips. Each Ship die enables shipping one good. Shipping is your primary VP engine.

walkthrough.title: Step Six: Return Dice
walkthrough.text: After all phases resolve, dice that were used go to your Citizenry, not back to your cup. Dice assigned to phases that were not chosen this round return to your cup for the next round. This is an important distinction. Used dice need to be recruited back into your cup by spending credits.

walkthrough.title: Step Seven: Recruit and Prepare
walkthrough.text: Between rounds, you can spend credits to recruit dice from your Citizenry back into your cup. Each die costs one credit to recruit. More dice in your cup means more options next round. Decide how many to recruit based on your credit supply and strategy. Then pick up your cup dice and prepare for the next round.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Wild Dice
walkthrough.text: Dice that roll the Wild face, shown as a dollar sign, are the most flexible. You can assign a Wild die to any phase, regardless of color. This makes Wild faces extremely valuable because they let you put dice exactly where you need them. Some tile powers also let you reassign dice from one phase to another, which works similarly.

walkthrough.title: Tile Construction Rules
walkthrough.text: When you draw tiles from the bag during Explore, they go under construction. A tile stays under construction until you place enough dice on it to meet its cost. You can add dice to a tile across multiple rounds, building incrementally. Once completed, the tile moves into your tableau. You can have multiple tiles under construction at once, but spreading dice too thin slows everything down.

walkthrough.title: Game End Triggers
walkthrough.text: The game ends when either trigger is met. First, if any player completes their twelfth tile in their tableau, the game ends after that round finishes. Second, if the VP chip pool runs out during shipping, the game ends after that round. Both triggers finish the current round before scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Add up three things. First, the VP value printed on every tile in your tableau. Second, any bonus VP from tiles that grant conditional endgame scoring based on your tableau composition. Third, all VP chips you accumulated during the game from shipping. The highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most dice plus credits wins.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Roll for the Galaxy
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three example players. Each player has a different faction with different starting dice and tiles. We will see all five phases in action and learn from some common beginner mistakes. Let's roll!

walkthrough.title: Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has a faction that starts with strong Explore capabilities. Player 2 has a military-focused faction with extra red dice. Player 3 has a production-oriented faction with blue and brown dice for goods. Each faction's dice and tiles push toward a different strategy.

walkthrough.title: Roll Your Dice
walkthrough.text: Everyone picks up their cup dice and rolls behind their screens. Player 1 rolls two Explore faces, one Develop, and one Wild. Player 2 rolls one Settle, two Develop, and one Ship. Player 3 rolls one Produce, one Ship, one Explore, and one Wild. Now each player needs to assign dice to phases and choose which phase to activate.

walkthrough.title: Assign and Select
walkthrough.text: Player 1 assigns the two Explore dice and the Develop die to their matching phases. They put the Wild die on Explore for extra tile draws. Player 1 selects Explore as their phase. Player 2 assigns dice to Settle and Develop, and selects Settle. Player 3 assigns dice to Produce, Ship, and Explore, and selects Produce.

walkthrough.title: Reveal and Check Active Phases
walkthrough.text: Everyone lifts screens. The selected phases are Explore, Settle, and Produce. That means Develop and Ship are not active this round, even though some players have dice assigned there. Those dice will return to their cups unused. Three phases will resolve in order: Explore, then Settle, then Produce.

walkthrough.title: Resolve Explore
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has three Explore dice. They draw three tiles from the bag and gain three credits. They look at the tiles, keep two that match their strategy, and return one to the bag. The kept tiles go under construction. Player 3 has one Explore die, so they draw one tile and gain one credit. They keep that tile. Player 2 has no Explore dice, so they skip this phase.

walkthrough.title: Resolve Settle
walkthrough.text: Player 2 has one Settle die and wants to start building a military world they have under construction. They place the Settle die on the world. The world needs three military dice total, so this is just the first step. It will take more rounds to complete. Players 1 and 3 have no Settle dice assigned, so they skip.

walkthrough.title: Resolve Produce
walkthrough.text: Player 3 has one Produce die and a production world already in their starting tableau. They place the Produce die on the empty production world. That die is now a good sitting on the world. Player 3 will need to ship this good in a future round to convert it into VP. Players 1 and 2 have no Produce dice, so they skip.

walkthrough.title: Return Dice
walkthrough.text: Dice that were used go to each player's Citizenry. Player 2's Develop dice were assigned to an inactive phase, so those return to Player 2's cup for free. Same with Player 3's Ship die. Remember: used dice cost credits to recruit back, but unchosen-phase dice return free.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Mistake: Ignoring the Citizenry
walkthrough.text: Player 1 used three dice this round, which all went to their Citizenry. But Player 1 forgets to recruit any of them back. Next round, Player 1 only has one die in their cup. They roll just one die and have almost no options. Meanwhile, Player 3 spent one credit to recruit two dice back and has a healthy cup for round two.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Recruit Your Dice
walkthrough.text: Dice stuck in your Citizenry are doing nothing for you. You need to actively spend credits to recruit them back into your cup. Fewer dice in your cup means fewer options and less output each round. Budget your credits carefully and always recruit enough dice to maintain your engine's momentum.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Player 2's Mistake
walkthrough.text: In round two, Player 2 selects the Develop phase even though they do not have anything they urgently need to develop. They chose it because they rolled two Develop faces. But Player 3 was about to select Develop anyway. Player 2 could have selected Ship instead, getting a free ride on Player 3's Develop selection while also activating a phase nobody else chose.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Read Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Selecting a phase that someone else is likely to choose wastes your selection. Instead, try to predict what phases opponents will activate and get a free ride on them. Then use your own selection on a phase that only you need. This opponent-reading skill is the key to advanced play in Roll for the Galaxy.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 Produces and Ships
walkthrough.text: Player 3 demonstrates the core engine loop. They produced a good last round. This round, they selected Ship and assigned a Ship die. They trade the good for two credits and one VP chip. The die that was sitting on the world as a good goes to their Citizenry. They immediately recruit it back for the next round. This produce-and-ship cycle is the heart of the VP engine.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Mistake: Over-Exploring
walkthrough.text: Player 1 loves drawing tiles. They have selected Explore three rounds in a row. They have six tiles under construction and have not completed a single one. Meanwhile, Players 2 and 3 have each completed two tiles and are building their tableaus. Having lots of options means nothing if you never build anything.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build What You Draw
walkthrough.text: Exploring is important for finding the right tiles, but you need to actually build them. Too many tiles under construction means your dice are spread thin across too many projects. Focus on completing one or two tiles at a time before exploring for more. A finished tile in your tableau is infinitely more valuable than five tiles under construction.

walkthrough.title: The Engine Takes Shape
walkthrough.text: By the end of round three, the players are settling into their strategies. Player 1 has finally started building and completed a development that grants bonus Explore draws. Player 2 has settled a military world and is working on a second. Player 3 has a smooth produce-ship cycle generating steady VP. The game is taking shape.

walkthrough.title: Round Summary
walkthrough.text: Each round follows the same flow: roll, assign, select, reveal, resolve, return, recruit. The key decisions are which phase to select and how to manage your dice economy between cup and Citizenry. As your tableau grows, your engine becomes more powerful and your options expand.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all five phases in action, learned the dice economy of cup versus Citizenry, and watched three common mistakes play out. Remember: recruit your dice, read your opponents' likely phase selections, and build what you draw instead of over-exploring.

walkthrough.title: What's Next
walkthrough.text: Before your next game, check out General Tips for strategy basics on building efficient engines. Reference the Appendix for a quick guide to dice colors, phase details, and tile types. And remember, you can ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Happy rolling!

walkthrough.title: Lean Into Your Faction
walkthrough.text: Your starting faction tile is not just a random starting position. It is a strategic direction. Each faction gives you specific dice colors, starting tiles, and abilities that favor certain strategies. A military faction starts with red dice that are biased toward Settle faces. A production faction starts with goods-producing worlds. Read your faction carefully and lean into its strengths rather than fighting against them.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Dice Economy
walkthrough.text: The dice economy is the engine within your engine. Dice in your cup get rolled each round. Dice in your Citizenry do nothing until recruited. Every die you use goes to the Citizenry and costs one credit to bring back. If you spend all your credits and cannot recruit, your cup shrinks and your output drops. Always budget credits for recruitment before spending on other things.

walkthrough.title: Complete Tiles Quickly
walkthrough.text: A tile under construction contributes nothing to your engine. A completed tile scores VP, provides powers, and might grant new dice. The faster you complete tiles, the sooner they start working for you. Focus your Develop and Settle dice on one project at a time rather than splitting across multiple tiles. Finishing one tile quickly is almost always better than slowly building three.

walkthrough.title: Build a Produce-Ship Engine
walkthrough.text: The most straightforward VP strategy is the produce-and-ship cycle. Settle production worlds, produce goods on them, then ship those goods for VP chips and credits. This cycle is reliable, repeatable, and self-funding because shipping generates the credits you need to recruit dice. If you are unsure what to build, production worlds are a safe default.

walkthrough.title: Predict Opponent Phase Selections
walkthrough.text: Since only chosen phases happen, predicting what your opponents will select is a huge advantage. If you think an opponent will select Produce, you can ride their selection for free and use your own selection on a phase nobody else will choose. Watch what tiles opponents are building and what goods they have. A player with goods on worlds is almost certainly going to select Ship soon.

walkthrough.title: Balance Tile VP and Shipping VP
walkthrough.text: There are two main sources of victory points: the VP printed on your tableau tiles and the VP chips earned from shipping. A pure shipping engine generates lots of chips but may end the game by depleting the VP pool before you have many tiles. A pure development rush fills your tableau fast and may trigger the twelve-tile end condition. The best strategies balance both, using tiles that enhance your shipping while also scoring well on their own.

walkthrough.title: Watch the End-Game Triggers
walkthrough.text: Keep track of how many tiles each opponent has in their tableau and how many VP chips remain in the pool. When either trigger is close, shift your focus from building to scoring. If you are behind on tiles, focus on shipping for VP chips. If you are behind on chips, rush to complete high-VP tiles. Being caught off guard by the game ending is a common beginner mistake.

walkthrough.title: Use Wild Dice Wisely
walkthrough.text: Wild dice are your most flexible resource. Do not waste them on phases where you already have plenty of matching dice. Instead, use Wilds to fill gaps. If you desperately need a Ship die but only rolled one, assign your Wild to Ship. If you need to complete a development this round, put the Wild on Develop. Wild dice should go where they have the most impact, not just wherever is convenient.

summary.title: Watch the End-Game Triggers

summary.title: Use Wild Dice Wisely
bullets: Assign Wilds where you have gaps, not where you have plenty
Fill critical needs: the 1 Ship die or the final Develop die
Maximum impact, not maximum convenience

walkthrough.title: The Shipping Engine
walkthrough.text: This is the most consistent VP strategy. Focus on settling production worlds and building developments that enhance your Ship phase. Look for tiles with consume powers that convert goods into multiple VP chips. The ideal engine produces two or three goods per round and ships them all for four to six VP per cycle. Blue novelty goods tend to be worth the most when traded.

walkthrough.title: The Development Rush
walkthrough.text: An alternative strategy is to rush developments for their high tile VP values and powerful abilities. Purple dice are biased toward Develop faces, making them ideal for this approach. Complete high-VP developments quickly to score their printed values and trigger their endgame bonuses. This strategy often triggers the twelve-tile end condition before shipping-focused opponents can accumulate enough VP chips.

walkthrough.title: Military Worlds
walkthrough.text: These require red dice to settle instead of generic Settle dice. This means you need to invest in gaining red dice early. The payoff is that military worlds often have powerful abilities and high VP values. Once you have three or four red dice, you can settle military worlds rapidly. Pair this with a production-shipping engine for a hybrid approach.

walkthrough.title: Dice Color Optimization
walkthrough.text: Each die color has a different face distribution that favors certain phases. White dice are balanced. Red dice favor Settle. Purple dice favor Develop. Blue, brown, green, and yellow dice favor Produce and Ship. As you gain new dice from completed tiles, choose colors that match your strategy. A shipping engine wants blue and brown dice. A development rush wants purple dice.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Game Pace
walkthrough.text: Advanced players manipulate when the game ends. If you are winning, you want the game to end quickly: rush your twelfth tile or drain the VP pool. If you are behind, you want to slow down and catch up. You can slow the game by focusing on building infrastructure instead of scoring VP. You can speed it up by shipping aggressively or completing cheap tiles to hit the twelve-tile threshold.

walkthrough.title: Phase Selection Bluffing
walkthrough.text: In advanced play, you can sometimes bluff your phase selection to manipulate opponents. If you have goods on worlds and opponents expect you to Ship, they might not select Ship themselves. Instead, you select Develop and use the free Ship phase that nobody else selected. This works best when you have Wild dice to flexibly assign to whatever phase you actually need.

walkthrough.title: Tile Synergy Chains
walkthrough.text: The strongest tableaus are built around tile synergies. A tile that grants bonus VP for every military world pairs perfectly with a military settlement strategy. A tile that lets you reassign dice combines with production tiles that need specific faces. When exploring, prioritize tiles that synergize with what you already have rather than tiles that are generically good. A mediocre tile that fits your engine is better than a great tile that does not.

walkthrough.title: Credit Management Mastery
walkthrough.text: Credits are the lifeblood of your dice economy. Advanced players never let their credit supply drop to zero because that means they cannot recruit dice. The ideal credit flow comes from shipping: trade a good for credits and VP, then use those credits to recruit the die that just came off the world. Build your engine so that shipping funds your recruitment automatically, creating a self-sustaining cycle.

summary.title: The Shipping Engine
bullets: Settle production worlds + Ship-enhancing developments
Consume powers → multiple VP per good
Target 2-3 goods/round, 4-6 VP/cycle
Blue novelty goods worth the most when traded

summary.title: The Development Rush
bullets: Rush high-VP developments with purple dice
Trigger 12-tile end before shippers accumulate chips
Endgame bonuses from development abilities

summary.title: Military Worlds
bullets: Require red dice to settle (not generic Settle dice)
Invest in red dice early for rapid military settlement
High VP values and powerful abilities
Pairs well with produce-ship hybrid

summary.title: Dice Color Optimization
bullets: White: balanced | Red: Settle | Purple: Develop
Blue/brown/green/yellow: Produce + Ship
Choose new dice colors matching your strategy

summary.title: Controlling the Game Pace
bullets: Winning → end game fast (rush 12th tile or drain VP pool)
Behind → slow down (build infrastructure, skip scoring)
Pace manipulation = advanced strategic lever

summary.title: Phase Selection Bluffing
bullets: Opponents expect you to Ship when you have goods
Select a different phase; ride their Ship selection free
Wild dice enable flexible bluffing

summary.title: Tile Synergy Chains
bullets: Prioritize tiles synergizing with existing tableau
Mediocre tile that fits > great tile that does not
Military VP bonus + military worlds = chain
Reassignment powers + production tiles = chain

summary.title: Credit Management Mastery
bullets: Never let credits drop to zero — blocks recruitment
Shipping funds recruitment automatically
Self-sustaining cycle: ship → credits → recruit → produce → ship

entries.definition: The starting die for all players. Has a balanced distribution of all six phase faces. Every player begins with several white dice in their cup.
Biased toward the Settle face. Required for settling military worlds. Gained from tiles that grant military capabilities.
Biased toward Produce and Ship faces. Represents novelty goods, which tend to be worth more when traded.
Biased toward Produce and Ship faces. Represents rare element goods for the production-shipping cycle.
Biased toward Produce and Ship faces. Represents genetic goods for the production-shipping cycle.
Biased toward Produce and Ship faces. Represents alien technology goods, often associated with high-value tiles.
Biased toward the Develop face. Ideal for strategies focused on building developments quickly.
A die face that can be assigned to any phase. Shown as a dollar sign. Extremely flexible and valuable for filling strategic gaps.
The container holding your active dice that get rolled each round. More cup dice means more options. Dice enter the cup via recruitment from the Citizenry or by being returned from unchosen phases.
The area where used dice wait to be recruited. Dice go here after being used in a phase. Recruit them back to your cup for one credit each. New dice gained from tiles also start in the Citizenry.
A player reference strip with five phase columns. Placed behind your screen. You assign dice to columns and select your chosen phase by placing a die on it.
A shield that hides your dice rolls and phase assignments from opponents. Lifted simultaneously when all players are ready to reveal.
Draw tiles from the bag and gain credits. Each Explore die lets you draw one tile and gain one credit. Keep tiles up to your Explore dice count. Kept tiles go under construction.
Build developments by assigning Develop dice to tiles under construction. Meet the die cost to complete the development and add it to your tableau.
Build worlds by assigning Settle dice (or red dice for military worlds) to tiles under construction. Meet the die cost or defense value to complete the world.
Create goods by placing Produce dice on empty production worlds. The die becomes the good token. Each production world holds one good maximum.
Convert goods into VP chips and credits by trading or consuming them. Each Ship die enables shipping one good. Uses consume powers on tableau tiles.
A tile type marked with a diamond icon. Built using Develop dice. Provides VP, tableau powers, and sometimes new dice when completed.
A tile type marked with a circle icon. Built using Settle dice or red military dice. Worlds can be production or non-production types.
A world that can produce goods during the Produce phase. Has a colored ring matching a good type. Can hold one good at a time.
A world with a red defense value instead of a die cost. Requires red military dice equal to its defense value to settle. Cannot be settled with generic Settle dice.
The state of a tile that has been drawn but not yet completed. Tiles under construction sit in your play area and accumulate dice until their cost is met.
Your completed collection of developments and worlds. Tiles in your tableau provide VP, powers, and contribute to endgame scoring. The game ends when any player has twelve tiles.
A die placed on a production world during the Produce phase. Represents a trade good that can be shipped for VP and credits. The good type matches the production world's color.
Currency used to recruit dice from Citizenry to cup. Earned through Explore and Ship phases. Essential for maintaining your dice economy.
Victory point tokens earned primarily through shipping goods. Come in one-point and five-point denominations. Pool depletion triggers game end.
Your starting tableau tile that determines initial dice, starting tiles, and strategic direction. Nine double-sided factions provide varied starting positions.
Some tile powers let you move dice from one phase to another during the assignment step. This increases flexibility by letting you use dice on phases that do not match their rolled face.
A tableau tile ability that converts goods into VP chips during the Ship phase. Different consume powers offer different VP conversion rates. Key to high-scoring shipping engines.
